A confidential and free pill testing service will be available at this year’s Schoolies at Surfers Paradise.

Queensland is the first Australian state to legalise drug checking, and afixed-site service at Bowen Hillsthat opened in April revealed risky synthetic stimulants in samples people thought were ecstasy and cocaine.

After getting their results, one in five people discarded the substance.

The Schoolies site will be near the entertainment precinct in Surfers Paradise and co-located with other health services.

Health Minister Shannon Fentiman said thousands of people died from drug-related deaths in Australia every year.

“One death is one too many,” she said.

“This additional service is all about empowering Schoolies with the information they need to make informed decisions that we hope will keep them safe.”

Thanks for joining us for theBrisbane Timeslive blog this Thursday afternoon. Here are some of the major stories that made headlines today.

A Brisbane man has apologised in court for assaulting a refereeafter an amateur game of Oztag erupted in claims of bias.

Sports betting has soared in popularity in Queensland andnow rakes in more money than all the poker machines in all the clubs across the state.

New Zealand’s independent inquiry into decades of abuse of children and vulnerable adults has released a blistering final report,finding the country’s state agencies and churches failed to prevent, stop or admit to the mistreatment of those in their care.

Four days after withdrawing from the presidential race,Joe Biden says the time is right to “pass the torch to a new generation”.

And we have welcomed two new trainee journalists to theBrisbane Timesoffices this week.Read all about them here– and you’ll be seeing their bylines on our home page soon.

A mother accused of killing her daughter told police she “did a lot of praying” when the eight-year-old stopped breathing after six days without her diabetes medication, a judge has heard.

Kerrie Elizabeth Struhs, 49, refused to enter a plea to the charge of manslaughter over the death of her daughter Elizabeth Rose Struhs on January 7, 2022.

Elizabeth died while lying on a mattress on the floor of her family’s home in Toowoomba, west of Brisbane, after days of suffering from a lack of insulin with symptoms of vomiting, unquenchable thirst, incontinence and loss of consciousness.

Detective Acting Sergeant Melissah Rose told a judge-only trial she responded to a report of a sudden death of a child on January 8, 2022.

During an interview out front of the family home, Rose recorded Kerrie Stuhs telling her Elizabeth’s father “experienced the power of God” and decided to stop giving her insulin.

“There wasn’t really any discussion. We just felt at peace. He told us that’s what he wanted to do,” Struhs said.

Elizabeth’s father, Jason Richard Struhs, 52 and Brendan Luke Stevens, the 62-year-old leader of a small Christian group called “The Saints”, are charged with the girl’s murder.

The Saints’ 11 other members allegedly present when Elizabeth died are charged with manslaughter.

All 14 defendants are self-represented and have refused to enter pleas.

Having embarked on a globe-trotting venture in her bid to become a more complete athlete, Tess Littleton will now be faced with the chance to cement herself among the Queensland Reds’ upper echelon.

The 24-year-old outside back will seek to come off the bench when her side clashes with Tonga on Friday, the first time a Super W outfit has ventured overseas.

The Toowoomba product travelled to 16 countries last year, which included playing stints with English rugby league’s London Broncos, as well as rugby clubs Hammersmith and Fulham.

“Rugby has always been No.1 for me. I first played for UQ when I was studying a few years ago and a job in Brisbane has brought me back,” Littleton said.

“It’s an amazing opportunity with the Reds ... it shows what’s possible when you are enjoying your rugby.”

An injury crisis in the Brisbane Lions’ backline could force coach Chris Fagan to take innovative measures in order to keep his side’s resurgent campaign charging.

Co-captain Harris Andrews (concussion) will join Jack Payne (foot), Tom Doedee and Darcy Gardiner (ACL ruptures) in the casualty ward for the forthcoming QClash with the Gold Coast Suns, leaving Fagan short on tall defenders against a towering rival forward line.

The likes of Ryan Lester and Darragh Joyce loom as the only genuine and seasoned options at the Lions’ disposal, and when asked if someone like tall forward Eric Hipwood would be considered for a shift back, Fagan said:

“I’m considering all tall players who can potentially play tall back there. Joe Daniher has played tall back before, so we could bring a forward in from the reserves and both of those guys back there – there’s a few things we can do there.

“It’s unlikely there will be a debutant. It’s tricky, but the ball doesn’t spend all the time in the air, does it? If we can get the ball to ground and work it out from there, it might turn into some sort of advantage for us.”

Dolphins fullback Trai Fuller’s remarkable rise is complete, with the late-bloomer inking a two-year deal which will keep him tied to Redcliffe until the end of 2026.

It marks the first time the 27-year-old has featured among a club’s Top 30 roster, having impressed when called upon to replace leading No.1 Hamiso Tabuai-Fidow on the back of a formidable seven years in the Queensland Cup.

Such has been Fuller’s form – averaging 173 running metres and more than four tackle busts a game to go with four tries and six linebreaks – the Dolphins could have faced a battle for his services long-term.

But the man himself admitted he never harboured any desire to hunt a new outfit, or move overseas.

“It’s special for me, especially with everything this club has done for me. It’s well worth the wait … I’ve always planned on staying here, whether it was in Cup or the chance to play NRL,” Fuller said.

“It never really crossed my mind to go anywhere else.”

Fuller has earned a chance in the Dolphins’ first-grade side even with Tabuai-Fidow back on deck, named on the bench for the second week running for Sunday’s clash with the Titans as he seeks to secure himself as the side’s interchange utility.

A Brisbane man has apologised in court for assaulting a referee after an amateur game of Oztag erupted in claims of bias.

Benson Leipau Pauga had approached Jason Fernie in the July 2022 game’s second half after his team accused the referee of favouring the opposition, a Brisbane court was told on Thursday.

After a verbal exchange, Pauga punched the 49-year-old on the jaw.

Fernie fell backwards hitting his head on the ground and convulsed, with blood on his mouth.

Pauga, now 34, left the sports ground while Fernie, a referee for 22 years, was taken to hospital with a brain bleed and concussion.

Read the full story.

Broncos enforcer Martin Taupau has pledged his desire to extend his career in Brisbane, insisting his recent form resurgence is not a case of winding back the clock.

The 34-year-old, who plays his 250th NRL game on Saturday against the Bulldogs, had reportedly been told he was unlikely to be extended beyond the end of his 2024, having been left out of the side for more than three months.

But Taupau’s comeback against the Warriors in round 17 announced he had plenty left in the tank. In what was his first appearance since the round three defeat to Penrith, he ran for 161 metres and made 23 tackles for no misses that day, while keeping his spot ever since.

“Prior to that [Warriors game] it was pretty hard, I just had to turn up for myself, train really hard and be patient. People say I’ve wound back the clock, but the clock hasn’t stopped ticking,” Taupau said.

“100 per cent [I want to stay], but the only thing I can control is playing great football, and things will work out.”

While star prop Payne Haas failed to complete Brisbane’s training session on Wednesday, Taupau allayed fears he would not be fit to take the field, claiming he was “perfectly fine”.

A coal miner who developed black lung due to his employers’ negligence has been awarded a landmark legal victory and a multimillion-dollar payout.

Craig Keogh, a machine operator at NSW and Queensland mines, became the first Australian to win a black lung case at trial, paving the way for other sufferers to make successful compensation claims.

The Dust Diseases Tribunal of NSW found the mines were negligent in exposing Keogh to coal dust because they did not take reasonable care to ensure he was not exposed to avoidable injuries.

Keogh, who was diagnosed with pneumoconiosis in 2018 and has battled mental-health issues since, was awarded $3.2 million in damages.

Downer Mining, CPB Contractors and Campbell Mining Services are all on the hook following the decision.

Keogh said he was never warned of the dangers dust from mines could pose and never wore a mask at work.

“It has basically destroyed me … physically I can’t do anything anymore, mentally there has been an impact on my thought process, my concentration, everything’s changed in my life,” he said.

“I’ll run out of breath very quickly, I’m always fatigued, it’s just not good – and it’s just going to get worse.”

Judge David Russell was convinced Keogh’s respiratory and psychiatric health meant he would never be able to work again, with evidence suggesting his respiratory condition will deteriorate.

Downer Mining and CPB Contractors have been contacted for comment.

Campbell Mining Services was voluntarily deregistered by ASIC in mid-2023.

Lifeline 13 11 14

beyondblue 1300 22 4636

AAP

A confidential and free pill testing service will be available at this year’s Schoolies at Surfers Paradise.

Queensland is the first Australian state to legalise drug checking, and afixed-site service at Bowen Hillsthat opened in April revealed risky synthetic stimulants in samples people thought were ecstasy and cocaine.

After getting their results, one in five people discarded the substance.

The Schoolies site will be near the entertainment precinct in Surfers Paradise and co-located with other health services.

Health Minister Shannon Fentiman said thousands of people died from drug-related deaths in Australia every year.

“One death is one too many,” she said.

“This additional service is all about empowering Schoolies with the information they need to make informed decisions that we hope will keep them safe.”

The prime minister has paid tribute to retiring minister Brendan O’Connor, who stayed in politics after the death of his wife.

“Brendan, of course, went through a great deal of personal tragedy in losing Jodi in 2018,” Anthony Albanese said.

“It would have been understandable if he stepped back at that time. He made a decision, along with Una, his lovely daughter, that he wanted to continue to make a contribution to our country.”

On departing minister Linda Burney, Albanese said she had lived an incredible life and suffered hardship, having lost her husband and son.

“Anyone who has the honour of calling Linda a friend understands the great strength behind her warmth and the courage that is the essence of her grace. The discrimination, hardship and loss she had to overcome in her life is more than most of us can comprehend,” Albanese said.

“Linda was born into an Australia where she was treated as a second-class citizen. And indeed was not recognised. Yet, her life is a record of profound firsts,” Albanese continued.

“The first Indigenous student to graduate from her teachers’ college. The first Indigenous person elected to the NSW lower house where she served as a minister but also rose to the heights of being the deputy Labor leader in New South Wales. She then went on to become the first Indigenous woman elected to the Australian House of Representatives.”
title: Brisbane news LIVE updates: House prices hit record; Cross River Rail test cancelled; Thieves take Olympic champ’s gear
description: Follow us for today’s updates in Brisbane and beyond.
